Hello there.

I looked the issues but I didn't find one that exactly matches this. If there is, I apologize for opening a duplicate.

The following code uses the most recent code in this repositories' master with python 3.6.5:

```python
>>> import toml
>>> dict_with_set = {'filled_set': set({1, 2}), 'empty_set': set({})}
>>> toml.dumps(dict_with_set)
'filled_set = {1, 2}\nempty_set = set()\n'
>>> toml.loads(toml.dumps(dict_with_set))
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/user/dev/tools/toml/toml/decoder.py", line 545, in load_inline_object
    _, value = candidate_group.split('=', 1)
ValueError: not enough values to unpack (expected 2, got 1)

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/user/dev/tools/toml/toml/decoder.py", line 419, in loads
    multibackslash)
  File "/Users/user/dev/tools/toml/toml/decoder.py", line 611, in load_line
    value, vtype = self.load_value(pair[1], strictly_valid)
  File "/Users/user/dev/tools/toml/toml/decoder.py", line 688, in load_value
    self.load_inline_object(v, inline_object)
  File "/Users/user/dev/tools/toml/toml/decoder.py", line 547, in load_inline_object
    raise ValueError("Invalid inline table encountered")
ValueError: Invalid inline table encountered

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/user/dev/tools/toml/toml/decoder.py", line 421, in loads
    raise TomlDecodeError(str(err), original, pos)
toml.decoder.TomlDecodeError: Invalid inline table encountered (line 1 column 1 char 0)
```

I don't know if this is considered in `loads`, but maybe it relates to the fact that `set`'s `__repr__` and `__str__` use braces when outputing:

```python
>>> s = set({1, 2})
>>> repr(s)
'{1, 2}'
>>> str(s)
'{1, 2}'
```

issue_title: Add support for asyncio to client
issue_body:
Python 3.4 adds support for [asyncio](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio.html), a native model for concurrent processing. This library should adopt it as one option for the event loop.

Some work has been done in the [asyncio branch](/jaraco/irc/tree/asyncio).

What remains is to identify what shortcomings exist, test the implementation, and provide directions on how to use this feature.

issue_title: fatal error: typedef redefinition with different types on OSX
issue_body: As explained in [this issue](https://github.com/bkaradzic/bgfx/issues/215#issuecomment-86517772), it seems OSX already includes GLsizeiptr and a few others. The same problem happens with glad.h as well. The workaround appears to be to use long instead of ptrdiff_t. [Here](https://github.com/tpaviot/oce/pull/380/files)'s another example.

When i was trying to read the variables information from the runtime like in the "Read PLC variable declaration" example from "https://infosys.beckhoff.com/english.php?content=../content/1033/tc3_adssamples_c/html/tcadsdll_api_cpp_sample10.htm&id=17583" in Python i have found that the "dataType" field was missed the "SAdsSymbolEntry" sructure. The next important thing is that ctypes strucures without "_pack_ = 1" field can have wrong size in bytes and field values in such structure may be wrong after the data were copied in that structure.
Here is the test script http://pastebin.com/qGMnkK2F
And its output on my PC http://pastebin.com/Q1hNFZQj

I'm totally into the "we only have one method" aesthetic but I think maybe it's gone too far :-)

Or maybe it's just that I don't understand what the difference is between `kernel.run(coro, shutdown=False)` and `kernel.run(coro, shutdown=True)`. The docs say "If shutdown is True, the kernel will cancel all daemonic tasks and perform a clean shutdown once all regular tasks have completed." But isn't that, like... what you always want?

But I'm confident that in the `kernel.run(shutdown=False)` version, calling `kernel.run` when what I want is for the kernel to not run, is super confusing. Surely this should be `kernel.shutdown()`.

Or `await curio.shutdown()` for that matter. I don't know why this is the one function in curio that doesn't go through the trap system.

issue_title: TLEN not correctly output in SAM
issue_body:

command I used:
./minimap2 -ax map-ont ref.fa some.fastq > aln.sam

Hello,

I have a set of contigs that I map against themselves. minimap2 correctly identifies the trivial self alignment but doesn't report other alignments. 
What I mean is that, the contig 1 matches with contig 1, which is both correct and trivial, but it should also matches with contig 4 (as reported by lastz and last, for example). However, minimap2 omits those matchings. 

The command I used is
`minimap2 -t 8 -x asm5 contigs.fa contigs.fa > contigs.paf `

Is it the expected behaviour? 

Thank you

@zjffdu I would not do this

```
def output_zeppelin():
    output_notebook(notebook_type='zeppelin')
    install_notebook_hook('zeppelin', partial(load_notebook, notebook_type='zeppelin'),
                          _show_zeppelin_doc_with_state, _show_zeppelin_app_with_state, overwrite=True)
```

I would suggest this instead:
```
install_notebook_hook('zeppelin', 
                      YOUR_OWN_LOAD_FUNCTION_BZKEP_HERE
                     _show_zeppelin_doc_with_state, 
                     _show_zeppelin_app_with_state, 
                     overwrite=True)
```

Then users can just do `output_notebook(notebook_type='zeppelin')` (i.e. no need for an `output_zeppelin`. basically,  you should:

* not rely on `output_notebook(notebook_type='zeppelin')` *inside* this repo, the idea is that that code currently built into bokeh specialized to zeppelin will be removed so every thing can be maintained her in one place. `output_notebook(notebook_type='zeppelin')` only works without "installing" because I left the current code in place until you can publish a better replacement here. But this repo should not rely on the code I left temporarily. 

  *After* you run `install_notebook_hook`, that is what will ultimately make `output_notebook(notebook_type='zeppelin')` "just work" for users. 

* Also not rely on `partial(load_notebook, notebook_type='zeppelin')` i.e. you should make a function *here* that does what that code does, then install *that*

issue_title: A Cross-Platform Terminal UI for howdoi
issue_body:
[LiftyNiftySubweb](https://repl.it/@tra38/LittleNiftySubweb) is a command-line script that is hosted on [repl.it](https://repl.it). When you click Run, it will install howdoi onto their Python instance and then allow you to decide what site you want to use howdoi on. Then you simply type in your query and get some responses. You can stop and restart the script to choose a new site to search.

I wrote this script because I wanted a cross-platform way to use howdoi without needing to install Python onto another machine or to turn howdoi into an executable program. However, this script will artificially juice howdoi's download metrics (due to needing to install howdoi every single time a new Python instance is spun up). I think this would be an acceptable trade-off though (especially as it ensures that LiftyNiftySubweb's version of howdoi is the most recent version).

issue_title: Inconsistent Travis issues with certain unit tests
issue_body:
Once in a while, e.g., every 10th time, Travis seems to complain about a certain unit test of the multi-layer perceptron. I couldn't find out what causes the problem but can't reproduce the issue on my local machine. Also, it works just fine via Travis ~90% of the time. I am wondering if it is related to the random state being set incorrectly, but since the random state is set equally for all classifiers inside the fit method that is imported from `_BaseSupervisedEstimator`, I don't think this is the issue here. I think it is something hardware specific maybe. 

As a current work around this problem, I changed the unit test to the following:


```python
def test_multiclass_gd_acc():
    mlp = MLP(epochs=20,
              eta=0.05,
              hidden_layers=[10],
              minibatches=1,
              random_seed=1)
    mlp.fit(X, y)
    assert round(mlp.cost_[0], 2) == 0.55, mlp.cost_[0]

    if round(mlp.cost_[-1], 2) == 0.25:
        warnings.warn('About 10% of the time, mlp.cost_[-1] is'
                      ' 0.247213137424 when tested via Travis CI.'
                      ' Likely, it is an architecture-related problem but'
                      ' should be looked into in future.')
    else:
        assert round(mlp.cost_[-1], 2) == 0.01, mlp.cost_[-1]
        assert (y == mlp.predict(X)).all()
```

issue_title: MySQL database create script should use innodb instead and bigger blob.
issue_body:
MySQL database create script should use innodb instead and a solution to be able to save more than 65535 bytes / BLOB for results (MEDIUMBLOB).

By using innodb, we can use  ROW_FORMAT=COMPRESSED to compress our results blobs. This is pretty essential for big crawls. Not to say innodb has better insert/update performance over myisam. Using MEDIUMBLOB/LONGBLOB over BLOB with ROW_FORMAT=COMPRESSED barely affect any performance.

And a normal BLOB (65535 bytes) for results column is too limiting.

A new, unique temporary directory should be used. This can be done using `tempfile.mkdtemp()` instead of hardcoding it to `os.path.join(gettempdir(), 'plyplus')`.

In my case, I have two users on the same filesystem running the same application (or could be two different applications both using plyplus). By running the application, a temporary directory is created with the first user's ID, which is unable to be read/written to by the second user.

I believe that the reduce_points algorithm incorrectly compares distances. The code you've written is the following:

    reduced_points = []
    for point in self.points:
        if reduced_points:
            distance = reduced_points[-1].distance_3d(point)
            if distance >= min_distance:
                reduced_points.append(point)
        else:
            # Leave first point:
            reduced_points.append(point)

    self.points = reduced_points

The first point is always kept. The distance between the first point and second point is compared to the threshold. If it's over the threshold, the second point is added. If not, the first point is compared to the third point. This is where the mistake is.

Measuring the distance from the first point to the third point doesn't follow the path, unless all the points follow a straight line. The best case is that the distance is longer than the accumulated distance following the path, and in the worst case, it stays under the threshold because the real path loops back close to another section of the track.

I'm not sure if this is the cause of the strange results I'm seeing, but when I take the following track...

![thun_1_raw](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/175080/7830069/f474177e-0448-11e5-8299-d9598b1e456c.png)

...and run `reduce_points(10)` on it, I get the following...

![thun_1_raw](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/175080/7830076/148fef74-0449-11e5-9416-a3e38aaa5738.png)

...which doesn't look correct. I'd love to know what your thoughts are on this.

You can find the GPX files in my repository here: https://github.com/jonblack/cmpgpx

Hello,

This is to discuss a solution (if a solution is even required) to the various warnings printed by the library. This often interfere with scripts, ruining output formating. In this case, messing up a progress bar:

```
11%|████▊                                      | 4/36 [00:01<00:08,  3.61it/s]no data to be resampled
no data to be resampled
 17%|███████▏                                   | 6/36 [00:01<00:06,  4.44it/s]no data to be resampled
 25%|██████████▊                                | 9/36 [00:02<00:06,  4.36it/s]no data to be resampled
 31%|████████████▊                             | 11/36 [00:02<00:05,  4.33it/s]no data to be resampled
 44%|██████████████████▋                       | 16/36 [00:03<00:04,  4.24it/s]no data to be resampled
 58%|████████████████████████▌                 | 21/36 [00:05<00:03,  3.94it/s]no data to be resampled
 61%|█████████████████████████▋                | 22/36 [00:05<00:03,  4.02it/s]no data to be resampled
 64%|██████████████████████████▊               | 23/36 [00:05<00:03,  4.04it/s]no data to be resampled
 67%|████████████████████████████              | 24/36 [00:05<00:02,  4.06it/s]no data to be resampled
 72%|██████████████████████████████▎           | 26/36 [00:06<00:02,  4.12it/s]no data to be resampled
 86%|████████████████████████████████████▏     | 31/36 [00:07<00:01,  4.06it/s]no data to be resampled
 89%|█████████████████████████████████████▎    | 32/36 [00:07<00:00,  4.10it/s]no data to be resampled
 97%|████████████████████████████████████████▊ | 35/36 [00:08<00:00,  4.13it/s]no data to be resampled
100%|██████████████████████████████████████████| 36/36 [00:08<00:00,  4.14it/s]
```

The particular library i am using (tqdm) has ways of dealing with print statements inside the loop by routing the output to a custom print function, but that means changing the print instruction itself. While i supposed that can be done inside mdfreader to deal with this particular progress bar library, i am certain it won't work in all cases.

I would argue that the best way to handle these types of messages is by returning a status message. In the case of mdfreader.resample(), the function could return the error message. If i am interested in knowing if there is any data to resample or not, i would store the result in a variable. If not, i can just not store it. This would not need any code changes in any function using this library. This might be difficult to implement seamlessly in functions which already return values (such as .getChannelData())

Another approach would be to have some sort of flag that puts the library in "quiet" mode. This would have the advantage of working seamlessly with all functions.

Sometimes, these warnings are expected and should be dealt with in code. If there is no data to resample and my script doesn't handle this correctly, it will still crash whether or not i get the warning.

Let me know what you think about this. It will obviously take some time to implement, but i think it's a good change.

issue_title: Deprecate asn_alts/allow_permutations in favor of new argument: asn_methods
issue_body:
asn_alts in asn.IPASN.lookup(), asn.ASNOrigin.lookup(), and ipwhois.IPWhois.lookup*() has a static first lookup method (DNS for IPASN, WHOIS for ASNOrigin). 

Deprecate asn_alts and add a new argument 'methods'. This will be an ordered list of ALL lookup methods, so you can skip the initial DNS/WHOIS attempts if needed.

The new 0.15.0 class ASNOrigin was designed this way for the sake of consistency until deprecated in IPASN and IPWhois.

Any help to troubleshoot would be very much appreciated, as I would love to use this package.

Thank you for your time.

I'm using TI BLE's SimpleBLEPeripheral code on the GATT server which gives a UUID

When sensortag.py is modified with the following

def _TI_UUID(val):

class SimpleGatt(SensorBase):
    svcUUID  = _TI_UUID(0xFFF0) # Simple Profile Servce UUID

issue_title: Getting pyshark packets from queue / pickle problems
issue_body:
Hello,

As I was trying to capture packets live using `pyshark` and do some multiprocessing, I encountered problems when getting a packet previously put in a `multiprocessing.Queue` or when I try to un-pickle it.  Here is the error I get:

> python2.7/site-packages/pyshark/packet/layer.py", line 48, in \__getattr__
> val = self.get_field_value(item, raw=self.raw_mode)
> (... multiple times ...)
> **RuntimeError**: maximum recursion depth exceeded while calling a Python object`.

This error comes whatever the `sys.recursionlimit` is set to (I stopped trying after raising it by a ten-factor).
I noted that this issue does not happen when using `Queue.Queue` so I suspect this is a problem with "reconstructing" the object when it's unpickled (as `multiprocessing.Queue` uses some kind of pickler, while `Queue.Queue` is a `collections.deque` under the hood).  

Is this a "normal" behavior and pyshark's packets cannot be pickled and un-pickled or is it a real issue ?

Here is the code I use to reproduce that issue
I am running a standard 2.7.9 python on RedHat 6.5

```python
import pyshark
import multiprocessing
import Queue
import cPickle as pickle

# Capture on eth0
interface = pyshark.LiveCapture(interface="eth0")

def queue_test(queue):
    """ Puts captured packets in a queue, then un-queue them and display """
    for packet in interface.sniff_continuously(packet_count=5):
        queue.put(packet)

    while not queue.empty():
        packet = queue.get()
        print "Packet {} {}".format(packet.highest_layer,packet._packet_string)


def pickle_test():
    """ Immediately pickle and unpickle the packet to display it"""
    for packet in interface.sniff_continuously(packet_count=5):
        pickled_packet = pickle.loads(pickle.dumps(packet, pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL))
        print "Packet #{}, {} {}".format(pickled_packet.highest_layer,pickled_packet._packet_string)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Try two types of queues
    normal_queue = Queue.Queue()
    process_queue = multiprocessing.Queue()

    # Runs fine
    #queue_test(normal_queue)

    # Both crash with a RuntimeError
    queue_test(process_queue)
    #pickle_test()
```

For cross-references, I had a [question posted on StackOverflow about this problem](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/28746662/pyshark-packets-queue-and-pickle-errors), but I am logging this issue here because it didn't get much attention.

Thanks you

issue_title: Add ability to "append" header tags separately (if necessary)

Hi. I would love to see the following code work:
```python
import freezegun, datetime

dates = [datetime.datetime(year, 1, 1) for year in range(1, 2000)]
with freezegun.freeze_time(lambda: next(dates)):
    print(datetime.datetime.now()) # would print the first of January "1
    print(datetime.datetime.now()) # would print the first of January "2
```
The idea is that in some test one may want that each `datetime.now` call return a different value he decided himself. This code is a simple example, but we can easily imagine lambdas more complicated.

What do you think? Would you accept pull requests for such a feature?

When freezegun is used together with threading, quite unexpected and hard to find things can happen.

Here is example:

```python
from Queue import Queue
from freezegun import freeze_time

@freeze_time('2016-01-04 16:49:59')
def test_me():
    queue = Queue()
    queue.get(block=True, timeout=0.1)
```

This will deadlock forever, even if `timeout=0.1` is specified, because `freezegun` patches `time`. Same thing happens for many other threading routines.

It is quite easy to fix:

```python
@freeze_time('2016-01-04 16:49:59', ignore=['threading', 'Queue'])
def test_me():
    queue = Queue()
    queue.get(block=True, timeout=0.1)
```

But at least for me it took quite a lot of time to figure this out.

I would suggest to add `threading` and `Queue` to `ignore` by default, or at least explicitly specify this in documentation.

Test script:

# Constants.

# Functions.
def remove(name):
    if name in ipdb.by_name.keys():
        print("removing %s" % name)
        ipdb.interfaces[name].remove().commit()

def cleanup():
    for i in range(RANGE):
        testgre_name = "testgre%i" % i
        testbr_name = "testbr%i" % i

        remove(testbr_name)
        remove(testgre_name)

# Setup.

Checked with todays's master.

When you create a multipath route:
```
# ip -6 route add ::ffff:9.8.7.6/128 table 64 nexthop dev eno1 weight 1 nexthop dev dhcp-s weight 1
```
it is shown as two routes by `ip route list`:
```
# ip -6 route list table 64
::ffff:9.8.7.6 dev eno1  metric 1024  pref medium
::ffff:9.8.7.6 dev dhcp-s  metric 1024  pref medium
```
but IPDB displays only one route:
```
>>> from pyroute2 import IPDB
>>> db=IPDB()
>>> for r in db.routes.tables[64]:
...     print(r)
... 
{'tos': 0, 'flags': 0, 'multipath': (), 'encap': {}, 'table': 64, 'dst_len': 128, 'pref': '00', 'proto': 3, 'ipdb_priority': 0, 'metrics': {}, 'dst': '::ffff:9.8.7.6/128', 'type': 1, 'family': 10, 'oif': 11, 'scope': 0, 'ipdb_scope': 'system', 'src_len': 0, 'priority': 1024}
>>>
```
If you delete one of these routes:
```
# ip -6 route del ::ffff:9.8.7.6/128 table 64 dev eno1
```
the other one is left in the kernel:
```
# ip -6 route list table 64
::ffff:9.8.7.6 dev dhcp-s  metric 1024  pref medium
```
but IPDB thinks that there are no routes:
```
>>> for r in db.routes.tables[64]:
...     print(r)
... 
>>> 
```
until you re-instantiate IPDB. After re-instantiation, it shows one route again. So on both occasions IPDB "sees" less routes than there are in the kernel.

issue_title: UnboundLocalError thrown for certain timeout values
issue_body:
Folks - quick question about some strange behavior we are seeing on the consumer side.

We made the following change, in particular dropping out `session_time_ms`:
```bash
@@ -44,7 +44,7 @@ def get_kafka_consumer(topic,group_id):
                              api_version=KAFKA_API_VERSION,
                              auto_offset_reset='latest',
                              enable_auto_commit=False,
-                             session_timeout_ms=300000)
+                             session_timeout_ms=5000)
     return consumer
```

Which resulted in our code throwing the following error:
```bash
Exception in thread Thread-3:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/threading.py", line 810, in __bootstrap_inner
    self.run()
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/threading.py", line 763, in run
    self.__target(*self.__args, **self.__kwargs)
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/multiprocessing/pool.py", line 380, in _handle_results
    task = get()
  File "<...>/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/kafka/protocol/pickle.py", line 32, in _unpickle_method
    return func.__get__(obj, cls)
UnboundLocalError: <unprintable UnboundLocalError object>
```

Looking at the code that throws the error:
```python
def _unpickle_method(func_name, obj, cls):
    for cls in cls.mro():
        try:
            func = cls.__dict__[func_name]
        except KeyError:
            pass
        else:
            break
        return func.__get__(obj, cls)
````

I assume that a `KeyError` causes execution to fall through without binding a value for `func`. Why does a timeout, or setting a time value too low, cause an `UnboundLocalError`?

I would like to help fix, but not sure what the cause is.

-Oscar

While using InotifyTree, I noticed that if you create and remove a directory very quickly, `event_gen()` fails. The reason for this is obvious: it sees the `CREATE` event and tries to install a new probe, which fails, as the target no longer exists.

InotifyTree should simply ignore such errors.

issue_title: Crash when non-ascii characters are used in symbols or sonames
issue_body:
At the bottom of every read from memory is the conversion from bytes to string with `.decode('ascii')`. This fails extremely loudly when there's a character >0x7e. Strings like this can occur naturally in e.g. elf files found on android systems. To reproduce just copy libc.so.6 and replace the libc.so.6 soname text with libc\xffso.6 or whatever.

Two possible solutions:
- return bytes instead of string
- replace `s.decode('ascii')` with `''.join(chr(c) for c in s)`

issue_title: Maya gains an extra second when parsing dates after the year 2243
issue_body:
Consider the following program:

```python
import datetime
import maya

d = datetime.datetime(2243, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1)
print(d)
print(str(d))

parsed_d = maya.parse(str(d)).datetime()
print(parsed_d)
print(str(parsed_d))

assert d == parsed_d
```

```
2243-01-01 00:00:00.000001
2243-01-01 00:00:00.000001
2243-01-01 00:00:00.000002+00:00
2243-01-01 00:00:00.000002+00:00
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "counterexample.py", line 15, in <module>
    assert d == parsed_d
AssertionError
```

This is a bug introduced somewhere in Maya – I see that Maya is using dateutil for the main machinery of its `parse` function, but if I try this with dateutil everything is fine:

```python
import datetime
from dateutil import parser

d = datetime.datetime(2243, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1)
print(d)
print(str(d))

parsed_d = parser.parse(str(d))
print(parsed_d)
print(str(parsed_d))

assert d == parsed_d
```

```
2243-01-01 00:00:00.000001

I'm wondering if anyone would benefit from from having a new test module for testing timezones for the systems under test?  

Scenario
---------

I built some new CentOS 7 machines and needed to set their timezones with ansible.  This works fine, but I want to make sure those timezones stay in sync with my code so I wrote a fixture like this and placed it in `conftest.py`

```
@pytest.fixture
def TIMEZONE(host):
    def on_call(expected):
        date_cmd = host.run('date +%Z')
        assert date_cmd.rc == 0
        assert date_cmd.stdout.strip() == expected.strip()
    return on_call
```

Then I have a test that looks like this

```
def test_timezone_is_UTC(host, TIMEZONE):
    system_info = host.system_info
    distro = system_info.distribution.lower()

    if distro == 'centos':
        if system_info.release == '6':
            TIMEZONE('UTC2')
            return
        if system_info.release == '7':
            TIMEZONE('UTC')
            return

    raise NotImplementedError('No timezone tests for {host}:{release}'.format(host=distro, release=system_info.release))
```

I don't like to write tests like this because it adds complexity and breaks the simple test rule, but I do think that logic may be better inside a `timezone` module inside testinfra?  I'd be happy to code it up and submit a pull request if anyone would agree it would help?  Please let me know what you guys/gals think?

issue_title: Crop by size give mismatch results between the groundtruth and original image.
issue_body: When I set the centre property to False, the groundtruth and the original image are cropped at different locations while the expected behavior is that both images are cropped at the same location.

Environment
Python: 2.7
pyodbc: 4.0.23
OS: UBUNTU (version16.4)
DB: SQL Azure(12.0.2000.8)
driver: Microsoft ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server

issue:
am getting below error while using multiprocessing.pool with pyodbc connection object.

File "/usr/lib/python2.7/multiprocessing/pool.py", line 567, in get
    raise self._value
TypeError: can't pickle pyodbc.Connection objects

This is not a bug report.  I think it's more of a feature request/troubleshooting request.  

I am working to get a Django project to talk with a DBISAM 4.21 database using the pyodbc and django-pyodbc software and can find little to nothing about how to make this work.  It seems to me that DBISAM is a relatively obscure proprietary database server software based on SQL-92 standards.  It is not listed as supported by pyodbc at this time.  I'd like to see if it's not supported by pyodbc because of it's obscurity or if there is a technical hurdle. 

The company that makes the software (Elevate Software) claims on their website that they support connections from non Delphi based projects using their provided ODBC driver (http://www.elevatesoft.com/products?action=order&category=dbisam&type=other).  I've been provided with Visual Basic code that apparently works with this ODBC driver provided by the vendor.

I'm trying to use the DBISAM 4.21 database because I am working on interfacing an existing software written in Delphi that stores it's data in the DBISAM database with a new Python 3 + Django 1.10 based software that is yet to be written.

We have a test environment with Windows 2012 R2 running the DBISAM server software.  It has the ODBC driver from the vendor installed along with the vendors Delphi based application.  We have also loaded Python 3.6 along with Django 1.10.5 so we can try and get a connection working. 

I am completely new to the world of DBISAM database technology AND completely new to the Python ODBC world so let me know if I can offer more details on anything to help the process.  

I am wondering if anyone has successfully initiated a connection using pyodbc to a DBISAM database using pyodbc and the ODBC driver from Elevate Software?  If so could you provide some help?

If not, it appears that this software will accept SDN connections.  Is there a good resource for learning more about how to formulate the proper syntax for one of these?  The software vendor we are trying to integrate with has zero experience in Python.  They can't help much.
The tiff module should be internal-only.  Use leading underscore to say as much.
Do we really need these?

issue_title: Textbox background missing in OS X
issue_body: When building for OS X, the textbox and other UI elements are not visible. This does not happen for Windows and Linux.

We have test failures under Windows that doesn't fail in other OSes. The failure was determined to originate from the `fast_forward()` function in `mtools/util/logfile.py`.

The cause is, `filehandle.seek(-2, 1)` doesn't seem to be seeking backward. Tracing the `filehandle.tell()` output, I can see that the file pointer doesn't change position (where it was supposed to be moving backward per loop iteration), leading into an infinite loop.

A "workaround" that I've found so far is to increase the amount of seeking, e.g. `filehandle.seek(-200, 1)` would pass the test.

MongoDB 3.6.0-rc0 is out so it would be good to test if there are any obvious changes that `mlaunch` should handle.

issue_title: Remove unused objects in an idf
issue_body:
I am currently working with an idf for a parametric simulation study. That idf contains all of the schedules needed (for convenient reference) and EMS objects needed across that whole parameter space. Depending on the parameter values for any particular case, the resulting idf can include numerous schedules and EMS objects that may not be used in that model. This throws out (trivial) warnings when the files run, and in general make looking through the idfs more complicated and potentially confusing than it needs to be.

It would be great to have a means of automatically removing unused objects in an idf file. This should be pretty trivial for most objects (e.g. schedules - search if it is referenced by any other idf), but there are edge cases. For example, objects that are required but that do not directly reference any other object, like Version, Building, etc.  

The advantage (other than tidying up the idfs) is that it could also prevent errors - for example if I accidentally include a pipe branch that isn't connected to anything, this could remove it, and save on debugging the file after a simulation attempt.
Make it work.

issue_title: [feature request] Unicode that could catch-out MySQL's default unicode settings
issue_body:
I'm posting this as a feature-request given our chat at pydatalondon but it is cool if it is too specific to include in the standard library.

By default MySQL supports 3 byte Unicode encoding (`utf8`) rather than 4 bytes to do it properly, you have to explicitly choose `utf8mb4` (https://mathiasbynens.be/notes/mysql-utf8mb4) and you'll probably do that after you've suffered data loss. Python's unicode isn't to blame, it correctly sends out things like the pile-of-poo character (`"\N{PILE OF POO}"`) and MySQL's defaults don't encode it.

You _could_ provide a variant of the `text` generator that favours harder unicode examples (e.g. stuff in the 2010 https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plane_%28Unicode%29#Supplementary_Multilingual_Plane) like the infamous pile of poo (http://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/1f64b/index.htm). This could be useful for testing both storage engines and web services (due to encoding data for the web).

The current `text` generator will presumably sample some of these, but maybe forcing some of the harder examples to be included would help folk flush out more bugs?

Sidenote - if anyone wants to play with this and discovers that they don't have font support then there are some details here (e.g. for Ubuntu): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emoji#Usage

Okay, so I've just spent the past 5 hours trying to sort out the imaging module, and failed miserably. I reckon this is something Rich is a lot better suited to, so I'll leave it to him. Nevertheless, I'll leave some notes on what I've done to the module and where I think it gets confusing:

- The imaging module basically consists of all the CCD data we use in the lens analysis. This is the Image from the CCD, as well as its Noise, BackgroundNoise, ExposureTime, PSF and potentially derived quantities like the SignalToNoise.

- Where things like an Image or Noise are alwyas 2D, the BackgroundNoise or ExposureTime could be a single float giving the value over the entire image, or a 2D image containing the noise / exposure time in each individual pixel.

- Previously, all unit conversions (e.g. x_pixel_to_x_arc_second) were module level functions, which require the pixel scale and image dimensions to be input into them. I have restructured this so that every class above (e.g. Image, Noise, etc.) inherit a DataGrid (e.g. units and conversions). This inheritance uses the pixel_scale and image dimensions for coordinate calculations internally.

- Previously, all classes inherited from np.ndarray via a __new__ method. I am happy for you to go back to this, I changed it as I didnt know how to code with that style properly. From what I could tell, this made it difficult for the different classes to have different inputs and behave in different ways, which they will depending on the data at hand.

- Every Data class (e.g. the Image, Noise, ExposureTime) requires their own input pixel_scale and can be padded / trimmed independently. However, for a CCD, these different parts of the data all come from the same CCD, so they should all have the same 'shared' pixel scale and coordinates. There should be some way of grouping the different Data's into one class (e.g. CCDData) that does this. 

- There are lots of different ways to set up each Data. For example, the Noise can be input, loaded from a fits file, estimated from an image, estimated from an Image + ExposureTime, estimated from an Image + Backgrond noise, etc. This may just require us to have lots of class methods for each circumstance.

- The specifics of handling each image in the point above is what makings the imaging module so hard to streamline. Ideally, one could just give the code a set of data and it runs, but each Data can be initialized in many different ways.

issue_title: Unhandled HTTP 401 from requests.get on tracks from artists that have disabled Soundcloud API access
issue_body:
At first I thought this was an error on all API attempts, maybe related to recent changes to Soundcloud from them adding the "Soundcloud Go" product.

I was trying to do:

```
~/projects/SoundScrape/soundscrape$ ./soundscrape.py -f https://soundcloud.com/zhu/in-the-morning
Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)
```

which is coming from the attempted JSON decoding of an HTTP 401:

```
(Pdb) requests.get(streams_url).json()
*** JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)
```

the 401 shows up from:

```
(Pdb) requests.get(streams_url)
<Response [401]>
```

However, a little reading at Soundcloud forums turns up this:
[https://www.soundcloudcommunity.com/soundcloud/topics/soundcloud-go-api-access](https://www.soundcloudcommunity.com/soundcloud/topics/soundcloud-go-api-access)
which says:
>Thanks for writing on our help forum. I've done some research in that area and it is actually up to the uploading artist / distributor whether their content is fully available via the API. 

I confirmed that another track randomly selected from my stream is perfectly downloadable, just as it's always been since I've been (happily) using Soundscrape.

So basically Zhu has locked down his track access via the API.  😢 

As far as the issue here with the code, maybe we could handle the 401 and provide a better error message than just letting it fall through to a cryptic `Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)`?

issue_title: mutually recursive discharge macaroons cause infinite recursion
issue_body:
The following code causes a RuntimeError exception to be raised due to infinite recursion:

    import pymacaroons
    import base64
    
    m1 = pymacaroons.Macaroon(location="", identifier="root-id", key="root-key")
    m1.add_third_party_caveat("bob",  "bob-caveat-root-key", "bob-is-great")
    m2 = pymacaroons.Macaroon(location="bob", identifier="bob-is-great", key="bob-caveat-root-key")
    m2.add_third_party_caveat("charlie", "bob-caveat-root-key", "bob-is-great")
    m2 = m1.prepare_for_request(m2)
    pymacaroons.Verifier().verify(m1, "root-key", [m2])

An attacker could use this as a denial-of-service attack.

issue_title: Issue with fomulas in validation of date ranges
issue_body:
Hi,

I am using XlsxWriter to do write xlsx sheets with validation for large amount of fields, some of these dates. I want it to be impossible to input dates in the future. For this I want to set the maximum in a date range to `TODAY()`

I am using Python version 3.52. and XlsxWriter 1.0.5 (master commit https://github.com/jmcnamara/XlsxWriter/commit/f9bcad9a85118cd9c88dc607062d6a02adb361ca)  and Excel version 2010.

Here is some code that demonstrates the problem, this doesn't work with the current code:

```python
#!/usr/bin/python3
# encoding: utf-8
import xlsxwriter
import datetime as dt

options= {'validate': 'date',
            'criteria': 'between',
            'minimum': dt.date(2010, 1, 1),
            'maximum': '=TODAY()',  
            'input_title': 'Date',
            'input_message': '''Can be from 2010-01-01 to today.''',
            'error_title': 'Error',
            'error_message': 'Not a valid date [2010-01-01, today]'
        }

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('Test.xlsx')
worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

worksheet.data_validation(first_row=0,
                              first_col=1,
                              last_row=0,
                              last_col=1,
                              options=options)
worksheet.write(0, 0, 'Date:')
workbook.close()

```

I've made a rudimentary fix, that runs this code and outputs a valid xlsx sheet:

https://github.com/jmcnamara/XlsxWriter/compare/master...paalge:date

I am unsure if this is a feature request or a bug...

issue_title: close() sets self.fileclosed before closing the file
issue_body:
I've frequently had the problem that, at the end of my script, when I try and close the workbook I've accidentally left the excel file I'm writing to open after checking the output is correct and it then fails with a permission error. I tried to solve this by introducing a loop with a try..except to catch the PermissionError but this doesn't work because in workbook.close it has these lines:
```Python
    if not self.fileclosed:
        self.fileclosed = 1
        self._store_workbook()
```
which mean that if `self._store_workbook()` fails then it's already set the `self.fileclosed` flag and won't try again. I believe it should be like this instead:
```Python
    if not self.fileclosed:
        self._store_workbook()
        self.fileclosed = 1
```
so that you can retry if the `self._store_workbook()` call fails. I have locally modified my copy and this appears to be functional.

@RondoChen 在Issue 1中提出

## 需求

现在我用python做了一个系统监控脚本
简单说就是每隔1分钟查一次数据库，然后把新增的内容发动出去
目前是简单地用一个while循环去查表然后用send发出去，可以说是单向的
现在想完善一下，想脚本能：
* 同时进行几个不同的监控方法
* 接收on 和 off的指令去对不同的监控功能进行启停

估计是要类似例子上面用上函数修饰符，不知道作者能不能给一点建议。

## 程序功能
* 存在三个模式且可增加：normal, special, off
* 对应每10， 5秒回复及不回复
* 通过与文件传输助手交互完成设置
* 交互命令为`Set mode as normal`
* 支持py2, py3

## 示例程序

```python
import re, time, threading

import itchat

def function_producer(modeName):
    pauseTime = {'normal'  : 10,
        'special' : 5, }.get(modeName, 5)
    def _function_producer():
        if modeName != 'off':
            itchat.send('Reply of %s.' % modeName, 'filehelper')
        time.sleep(pauseTime)
    return _function_producer
FUNCTION_DICT = {
    'off'     : function_producer('off'),
    'normal'  : function_producer('normal'),
    'special' : function_producer('special'), }
KEY_WORD_REGEX = re.compile('Set mode as (\S+)')
MODE_FLAG = 'off'
def monitor_reply():
    while 1: FUNCTION_DICT[MODE_FLAG]()

@itchat.msg_register('Text')
def monitor_interact(msg):
    if not (msg['ToUserName'] == 'filehelper' and
        KEY_WORD_REGEX.match(msg['Text'])): return
    global MODE_FLAG
    modeFlag = KEY_WORD_REGEX.findall(msg['Text'])[0]
    MODE_FLAG = modeFlag if modeFlag in FUNCTION_DICT.keys() else 'off'
    itchat.send('Mode is set as %s.' % MODE_FLAG, 'filehelper')

itchat.auto_login(True)

monitorThread = threading.Thread(target=monitor_reply)
monitorThread.setDaemon(True)
monitorThread.start()

itchat.run()
```

Something I snuck into my pull request was a change to the order in which "searchmoves" is appended to the command string in the uci.  I found that searchmoves was clobbering other parameters like depth and movetime when appended first, as in the current code.  In other words:  

```go searchmoves [moves] depth 20 movetime 5000```

was ignoring the depth and movetime values completely.  I changed it to:  

```go depth 20 movetime 5000 searchmoves [moves]```

then it worked fine.  I changed the order of the commands in 00ee993c3f921f94286de2f540bc7bf7ab6c4170, which is currently causing an AssertionError due to a syntax check (uci.py, line 607) not allowing that order of commands. It would be a great addition to have an IO adapter for speech-to-text and text-to-speech. This would allow people to use ChatterBot in more situations and with less work.

- [ ] STT Adapter
- [ ] TTS Adapter

This is a pretty complicated addition, but the Jasper project does a great job implementing STT and TTS functionality. Some information about their user interaction ( how it can be configured ) can be found here http://jasperproject.github.io/documentation/configuration. The code for the STT and TTS engine(s) they implement can be found here https://github.com/jasperproject/jasper-client/blob/master/client/stt.py and https://github.com/jasperproject/jasper-client/blob/master/client/tts.py.

The implementation would be done in steps, with single-engine configuration coming first followed by multi-engine configuration.

- [ ] Single-engine configuration. In this, we would only allow the use of a single STT & TTS engine that we hard code into ChatterBot
- [ ] Multi-engine configuration. In this, we would allow multiple STT & TTS engines that the user can pick.

ChatterBot currently has a [few tie breaking methods](https://github.com/gunthercox/ChatterBot/blob/master/chatterbot/adapters/logic/mixins.py) that can be used to determine how it selects a response when there are multiple equivalent possible responses.

issue_title: Some common PIP package metadata missing
issue_body: See [on pydigger](https://pydigger.com/pypi/krakenex).

We came up with a neat feature idea at PyCon: using magic-wormhole to set up SSH pubkeys.

The use case is that Alice owns a computer, and wants to give Bob SSH acccess to it. Either Alice is root on the host and she's setting up a new account for Bob, or Alice is a normal user (logged in already) and is trying to add her own pubkey.

Alice runs something like `wormhole add-ssh`, maybe as `wormhole add-ssh --user=bob`. Then Bob runs `wormhole send-ssh`. The add-ssh command generates and displays a wormhole code. The send-ssh command looks in `~/.ssh/`, finds your pubkeys, and asks you which one you want to send, then accepts the wormhole code, and sends the pubkey. When add-ssh receives the pubkey, it appends it to `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` of the given user account.

issue_title: Doubt about model dict conversion behavior
issue_body:
A few days ago, I tried to convert a model instance to a dict, using the syntax `dict(model)`, and I got `TypeError`. The scenario is described below:

```python
import typing
from dateutil import parser

from simple_model import Model


class MyModel(Model):
    name: str = ''
    timestamp: typing.Any = None

    def __post_init__(self):
        self.clean()
        
    def clean_timestamp(self, value):
        return parser.parse(value)

data = {'name': 'Name', 'timestamp': '2018-02-05T10:00:01.000001Z'}
model = MyModel(**data)

dict(model)  # here the error occurs 
```

So, the `TypeError` exception is raised,  because simple model calls the method `self.clean()` during the conversion for a dict, and it tries to convert the timestamp attribute to a datetime again, but in this case, it already a datetime. 

I would like to know if this behavior is what you expected when you created this resource, or if it is a thing that needs to be fixed. I can verify the value type on `clean_timestamp` method, but I don't know  if it's the better approach.

issue_title: uri in extract_params() is not properly urlencoded
issue_body:
I've bumped into this problem with `extract_params()` today as we receive some requests that are not properly urlencoded.

I see `uri` is being generated calling `_get_uri_from_request`, however, I don't see how that is supposed to be retrieving a properly urlencoded `query_string` from `request`. In my case, using `request.url` gives me properly urlencoded urls while  `_get_uri_from_request` doesn't.

Delving into Werkzeug I found that `request.query_string` ([code](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/werkzeug/blob/d4e8b3f46c51e7374388791282e66323f64b3068/werkzeug/wrappers.py#L599)) returns a raw bytestring which is clearly not urlencoded, while `request.url`([code](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/werkzeug/blob/d4e8b3f46c51e7374388791282e66323f64b3068/werkzeug/wrappers.py#L560)) seems to be taking care of it ultimately with `get_query_string` ([code](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/werkzeug/blob/d4e8b3f46c51e7374388791282e66323f64b3068/werkzeug/wsgi.py#L210))

issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
Running the `LocalBest` optimizer does not return the best cost of the optimization process. If you run the script below the follwing output is given:

```python

   2.42419718]

INFO:pyswarms.single.global_best:Iteration 26/100, cost: 1.0102225946801522

[0.9740844  0.97442641 0.97406909 0.9744485  0.97396594 0.97411942]
```

**To Reproduce**
Run the following script:
```python
import pyswarms as ps
import numpy as np
from pyswarms.utils.functions import single_obj as fx

# Set-up hyperparameters
import pyswarms as ps
import numpy as np
from pyswarms.utils.functions import single_obj as fx

# Set-up hyperparameters
options = {'c1': 0.5, 'c2': 0.3, 'w':0.9, 'p':1, 'k':1}
bounds = (np.array([-10,-10]),np.array([10,10]))
# Call instance of PSO
optimizer = ps.single.LocalBestPSO(n_particles=6, dimensions=2, options=options, bounds=bounds)
# Perform optimization
best_cost, best_pos = optimizer.optimize(fx.levi_func, iters=100, verbose=3, print_step=25)
print(optimizer.swarm.pbest_cost)


# Call instance of PSO
optimizer = ps.single.GlobalBestPSO(n_particles=6, dimensions=2, options=options, bounds=bounds)
# Perform optimization
best_cost, best_pos = optimizer.optimize(fx.levi_func, iters=100, verbose=3, print_step=25)
print(optimizer.swarm.pbest_cost)
```

The `GlobalBest` class returns the best cost it found while the `LocalBest` class does not.

**Expected behaviour**
The `LocalBest` class should return the best cost it found as well.

 - PySwarms Version v0.2.1

issue_title: JSONField default passed to SQL create column query
issue_body:
Summary: Migrations created to add a JSONField include default, causing query to fail.

Django Version: Django 1.9.2-8

Database and version used: MySQL 5.7.11

Version: Django-MySQL 1.0.1

Hi,
I've been having some inconsistent behaviour with this so sorry if this is confusing. In the current situation I'm stuck in, I have model that was created with a JSONField without any problems, and I'm trying to add another JSONField. It keeps failing because the migrations tries to set a default on the field, which MySQL doesn't allow:
(`django.db.utils.OperationalError: (1101, "BLOB, TEXT, GEOMETRY or JSON column 'tables' can't have a default value")`)
I think I know why this is happening: https://github.com/django/django/blob/6bf7964023487f2a352084e74aca27aecb354d6c/django/db/backends/mysql/schema.py#L28
That method tells migrations to leave out the default for certain fields, and I believe JSONField would now need to be included among those, as the django_mysql JSONField implicitly sets `dict` as its default.

I can't immediately see how this could be rectified here. Do you think it requires a bug report for Django? Or maybe I've overlooked something.

I know I've left out full tracebacks and stuff but that's just because I'm pretty sure I've located the issue above. Let me know if you'd like more.

issue_title: util.get_int() broken on OS X / Python 3.5
issue_body:
The example code of Issue #65 fails on OS X 10.13.3 (snap7 v1.4.2, python-snap7 v0.9) also due some sign handling confusion.

```
# python3 test.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 8, in <module>
    Data_Get = snap7.util.get_int(DB1, 0)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/lib/python3.5/site-packages/snap7/util.py", line 148, in get_int
    value = struct.unpack('>h', struct.pack('2B', *data))[0]
struct.error: ubyte format requires 0 <= number <= 255
```

while the test case that was added after fixing Issue #65 (as all other test_util tests) succeed

```
# cd test
# python3 test_util.py
.............
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 13 tests in 0.004s

OK

```

The following shortend test2.py shows that the issue lies again in the encoding of integers larger than 127

```
import snap7
server = snap7.server.Server(log=True)
DB1 = (snap7.snap7types.wordlen_to_ctypes[snap7.snap7types.S7WLByte]*4)()
server.register_area(snap7.snap7types.srvAreaDB, 1, DB1)

for Data_Set in (0, 127, 128):
    snap7.util.set_int(DB1, 0, Data_Set)
    Data_Get = snap7.util.get_int(DB1, 0)
    print("%6d %6d %s" % (Data_Set, Data_Get, (Data_Set==Data_Get)))

# python3 test2.py
     0      0 True
   127    127 True
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test2.py", line 8, in <module>
    Data_Get = snap7.util.get_int(DB1, 0)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/lib/python3.5/site-packages/snap7/util.py", line 148, in get_int
    value = struct.unpack('>h', struct.pack('2B', *data))[0]
struct.error: ubyte format requires 0 <= number <= 255
```

Replacing the call `snap7.util.get_int(DB1, 0)` by the following modified `get_int(DB1, 0)` succeeds

```
import struct
def get_int(_bytearray, byte_index):
    """
    Get int value from bytearray.

    int are represented in two bytes
    """
    data = _bytearray[byte_index:byte_index + 2]
    data[1] = data[1] & 0xFF
    value = struct.unpack('>h', struct.pack('2B', *data))[0]
    return value

# python3 test2.py
     0      0 True
   127    127 True
   128    128 True
```

Accessing a server via TCP using a snap7 python client to read an integer via `snap7.util.get_int` that has been set on the server via `snap7.util.get_int` to a value larger than 127 works as expected.

issue_title: Sugestion: Return orriginal image if resize is same size
issue_body:
I noticed that when using a resize size that was the same size as the original file or when specifying a larger size with `upscale=False`, that a hashed cache file was still created.

I haven't delved into the code enough so I don't know if the cached file is a direct copy but even if it is, at best Flask-Resize is creating excess duplicate files, which could be a problem on a large image cdn and, at worst, is performing excess processing as well to produce the same image, which results in extra cpu usage and possibly a loss in quality.

I would recommend, if no other transformations of the image need to be performed and the requested size is the same as the original size, then Flask-Resize should serve the original image. perhaps add another possible parameter and call it `force_cache` or similar, and default it to `False`; setting the parameter to `True` will then force Flask-Resize to produce a cached image, even if size is the same as the original.

issue_title: Empty user (and comments) field for revisions created outside admin
issue_body:
I'm using the `'reversion.middleware.RevisionMiddleware'` middleware setting along with `reversion.register(MyModel)` after every model definition.
All my models have a `created_by` and `updated_by` field.

For objects that are updated through the Django Admin, I get a User and Comments field that is properly filled in.
However, for objects that are created or updated through my own non-admin views, the revision is created (done by the middleware) but there is no User or Comment information. I'm especially interested in the User part.
When having a look at the revisions in the admin, a dash (`-`) is displayed in the User and Comments column for those non-admin created/modified objects.

Any idea on what the proper way is to get proper tracking who changed the object, outside the admin?

Note that the `created_by` and `modified_by` fields seem to be tracked by reversion:

issue_title: method that takes a netloc instead of an URL?
issue_body:
I have some code for which url parsing is performance-sensitive, and I'm trying to reduce the number of times the full url is parsed. I'm also hoping to have one urlparser so that there's no way any url would get parsed one way by one module and a different way by another.

tldextract currently only takes an url as an argument. If I already know the netloc, then it'd be nice to have a different function to extract from that. It wouldn't make the tldextract code too ugly.

Alternately, the existing code extract() happens to do the right thing, albeit with a little extra expense, for extract('example.com').  It is not documented or tested that this works. I am willing to bet that there are some users of your code mistakenly depending on this functionality!

What do you think should be done? You might say "nothing", that's fine. I'm willing to take a stab at implementing if you think something should be done.

issue_title: RequestParser doesn't handle booleans submitted as 'false' correctly
issue_body:
When the RequestParser is given an argument of type `bool`, it parses _any_ value as `True` and only the absence of the parameter as `False`. If the argument  is default=`True`, it's impossible to actually get a false value.

Code demonstration:

``` python
from flask import Flask
from flask_restplus import Resource, Api

app = Flask(__name__)
api = Api(app)

test_parser = api.parser()
test_parser.add_argument('foo', type=bool, default=False)
test_parser.add_argument('bar', type=bool, default=True)

@api.route('/hello')
class HelloWorld(Resource):
    @api.expect(test_parser)
    def get(self):
        args = test_parser.parse_args()
        return dict(args)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(debug=True) 
```

The swagger docs give you a nice dropdown of [blank], 'true', 'false'. Using it gives the following:
- `http://127.0.0.1:5000/hello`
  - `{"bar": true, "foo": false}`
- `http://127.0.0.1:5000/hello?foo=true&bar=true`
  - `{"bar": true, "foo": true}`
- `http://127.0.0.1:5000/hello?foo=false&bar=false`
  - `{"bar": true, "foo": true}`

Considering the generated swagger documentation tells you to use `foo=false`, the RequestParser should interpret the string 'false' as the boolean False. 

I'm guessing under the hood it's doing something like bool("false"), which evaluates as True?

Attempted using Python 2.7.5, flask-restplus both 0.8.6 and 0.9.2

Is there a specific reason why `django-address` doesn't use [django-countries](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/django-countries) for the Country model? The package ships all known countries including ISO-3166-1 codes

Is there a particular reasons why this shouldn't work with 1.10?

Thank you.

issue_title: Incorrect escaping on queries using LIKE
issue_body:
Hi, I am using django 1.10 with pyodbc/freetds to connect with Sqlserver.

I need to run a query similar to this:
```sql
SELECT *
FROM users
WHERE email LIKE '%@[gb]mail.com'
```

In Django ORM I translate this as:
```python
queryset = User.objects.filter(email__contains='[gb]mail.com')
```

When I print the generated SQL I get:
```
print(queryset.query)
> SELECT [users].[id], [users].[email] FROM [users] WHERE [users].[email] LIKE '%[[]gb]mail.com%' ESCAPE '\'
```

As you can see, the first `[` is escaped, making the `LIKE` invalid.
Grepping the source code it seems to me that the escaping most likely happes in [operations.py](https://github.com/michiya/django-pyodbc-azure/blob/azure-1.10/sql_server/pyodbc/operations.py#L440-L443), where any of `]`, `[`, `%` and `_` are escaped.

I created a PR in the [pytest-json](https://github.com/mattcl/pytest-json/pull/8) project which would allow the use of the plugin from code rather than saving to a file. Since that project is not maintained would there be interest and adding similar functionality to pytest-json-report?

This would be helpful when running pytest in code:

```
cmds = shlex.split(args)
plugin = JSONReport()
result = pytest.main(cmds, plugins=[plugin])
plugin.report # report dict
```

I can make a PR but wanted to gauge interest first.

issue_title: ValueError when text in column is too long
issue_body:
When creating a xls report and the the text inside one of the cell in the table is too long, you get a ValueError because _column width (602112) not an int in range(65536)_.

The function _get_xls_col_width_ in the _xlwt_converter_ must not return a value higher than 65535. The _set_width_ method of the _Column_ class of _xlwt_ will raise the ValueError.

A possible fix to this would be to return the min value between the current approximation, and the max value allowed by _xlwt_.

I see that this is a more up-to-date fork of python-bloomfilter, but why not just have a PR to merge these changes back into the original repo? Also, would it be possible to have this available on the conda repo (conda-forge, probably)?

```
staff = QuerySetFilterJob(models.User).filter(is_staff=True)
```

When I executed the above code, here is error: 
`
 'QuerySetFilterJob' object has no attribute 'filter' 
`
I think it should be: 

```
QuerySetFilterJob(models.User).get(is_staff=True)
```

If the choices are defined like this,
```
    CHOICE1 = '1'
    CHOICE2 = '2'
    CHOICE3 = '3'
    CHOICE4 = '4'
    ANSWER_CHOICES = ((CHOICE1, 'A'),
                      (CHOICE2, 'B'),
                      (CHOICE3, 'C'),
                      (CHOICE4, 'D'))

```
then the __str__  method returns None, None, None ..   

it can be easily fixed by replacing 
`[msgl.choices.get(int(i)) if i.isdigit() else msgl.choices.get(i) for i in msgl]
`

with 

`[msgl.choices.get(i) for i in msgl.choices if i in self.msgl]
`

I think there may be a regression in 0.2.5, which seems to wait for 5s on all shutdown for me.

[This code](https://github.com/jruere/multiprocessing-logging/blob/7fd2dcd74fe4a1f0f024ba4daa8be464bee66297/multiprocessing_logging.py#L66) should probably not execute on the receiving side based on [the documentation](https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/multiprocessing.html#multiprocessing.Queue.join_thread); indeed the docs suggest it's not needed on the sending side either, since it's the default behaviour.

For reference, here's how logbook does it:
https://github.com/getlogbook/logbook/blob/9d81786f505a5edabc14cfb2b7af6567c219e38e/logbook/queues.py#L493
...but there's no decent example to show how to wire those two together!

My guess is that there's some thread management issue lurking in multiprocessing-logging or there was a process being managed badly. Thoughts?

issue_title: Allow overriding `universal_newlines` when running a subprocess
issue_body:
Could you possibly modify `run_subprocess()` to something like this?

```python
    def run_subprocess(self, command, *arguments, **options):
        stdin = ''
        if 'stdin' in options:
            stdin = options['stdin'].read()
            options['stdin'] = subprocess.PIPE
       if 'universal_newlines' not in options:
           options['universal_newlines'] = True
        p = subprocess.Popen([command] + list(arguments),
                             stdout=subprocess.PIPE, stderr=subprocess.PIPE,
                             **options)
        stdout, stderr = p.communicate(stdin)
return RunResult(p.returncode, stdout, stderr)
```

It would be really convenient to be able to override this option.

issue_title: Bad error handling regarding event listener signature
issue_body:
Let's consider the following setup: we use `socketio` with some `asyncio`-based server (like `sanic`). We subscribed to some custom message and expect just one data argument for it:
```python
from sanic import Sanic
import socketio

app = Sanic(__name__)
sio = socketio.AsyncServer(async_mode='sanic')
sio.attach(app)

@sio.on('custom message')
async def on_custom_message(sid, data):
    print('Got data {} from sid {}'.format(data, sid))

app.run()
```
Now we connect to this server and send message with incorrect data values count:
```js
var io = require('socket.io-client');
var socket = io();
socket.emit('custom message', 1, 2);
```
Here is what we get in the server's logs:
```
2017-06-20 00:03:30 - (sanic)[INFO]: Goin' Fast @ http://127.0.0.1:8000
2017-06-20 00:03:30 - (sanic)[INFO]: Starting worker [29888]
2017-06-20 00:03:32 - (network)[INFO][127.0.0.1:34022]: GET http://localhost:8000/socket.io/?EIO=3&transport=polling&t=Lp2Akw4&b64=1  200 117
yo
2017-06-20 00:03:32 - (sanic)[ERROR]: Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sanic/app.py", line 471, in handle_request
    response = await response
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/engineio/asyncio_server.py", line 135, in handle_request
    socket = self._get_socket(sid)
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/engineio/server.py", line 411, in _get_socket
    raise KeyError('Session is disconnected')
KeyError: 'Session is disconnected'

2017-06-20 00:03:32 - (network)[INFO][127.0.0.1:34028]: GET http://localhost:8000/socket.io/?EIO=3&transport=polling&t=Lp2Akwd&b64=1&sid=68f480e234f546f980340d107e3cda62  500 144
2017-06-20 00:03:32 - (sanic)[ERROR]: Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sanic/app.py", line 471, in handle_request
    response = await response
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/engineio/asyncio_server.py", line 137, in handle_request
    packets = await socket.handle_get_request(environ)
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/engineio/asyncio_socket.py", line 73, in handle_get_request
    return await getattr(self, '_upgrade_' + transport)(environ)
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/engineio/asyncio_socket.py", line 120, in _upgrade_websocket
    return await ws(environ)
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/engineio/async_sanic.py", line 122, in __call__
    await self.handler(self)
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/engineio/asyncio_socket.py", line 138, in _websocket_handler
    await self.send(packet.Packet(packet.NOOP))
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/engineio/asyncio_socket.py", line 53, in send
    raise IOError('Socket is closed')
OSError: Socket is closed

2017-06-20 00:03:33 - (network)[INFO][127.0.0.1:34030]: GET http://localhost:8000/socket.io/?EIO=3&transport=polling&t=Lp2Al5F&b64=1  200 117
2017-06-20 00:03:33 - (network)[INFO][127.0.0.1:34034]: GET http://localhost:8000/socket.io/?EIO=3&transport=polling&t=Lp2Al5Q&b64=1&sid=b13efb25fbd945f580bf7e5333623304  200 3
```

As a result, original exception (`on_custom_message() takes 2 positional arguments but 3 were given`) is not displayed at all, the only thing suggesting what happened is a mysterious `yo` message which is followed by some internal errors.

I think we could at least print basic exception information before disconnecting socket (see `asyncio_server.py` line `164`), because disconnection itself (together with its event handlers) may produce some other errors which will not allow us to see the original error.

issue_title: Check for driver latest version once a day
issue_body:
Here is an idea.

## What?

Cache not only binary of the driver but the latest version number as well. And update this number, let's say once a day.

## Why?

How frequently drivers are updated? Not very much.
But we waste time on each test run for requesting the latest version from the internet.
Such caching can speed up testing.

Section for PhantomJS has an typo
----

`driver = webdriver.PhantomJS(PhantomJsDriverDriverManager().install())`

should be

`driver = webdriver.PhantomJS(PhantomJsDriverManager().install())`

issue_title: Objects in results array should not be nullable
issue_body:
This a minor but annoying issue when you use a front end tool like Apollo because it results in weaker typing.
It affects graphene-django as well, but I have more confidence in graphene-django-extras to fix it ! :-)
Here it is: results should not be of the form `results: [UserType]` but `results: [UserType!]` since returned objects in the array can't be null.
I don't know if it's somehow related, but the same issues applies to related objects resulting from foreign keys or M2M.

EscapeAll escapes placeholder chars STX and ETX which are used to in the Markdown conversion process.  These should probably not get escaped.  This affects Critic, but also can affect other things.

Also, critic in code blocks aren't having tags inserted.  Critic tag insertion should be moved after raw HTML insertion.

issue_title: LZF encoding bit is wrong in redis-2.8.13
issue_body:
"""
Compressed Strings
First read the section “Length Encoding”, specifically the part when the first two bits are 11. In this case, the remaining 6 bits are read.
If the value of those 6 bits is 4, it indicates that a compressed string follows.
"""
Hint above is not correct.
In fact, in version 2.8.13, it is 'c3' means lzf compressed instead of 'c4'.
So "If the value of those 6 bits is 4" should change to "If the value of those 6 bits is 3"

issue_title: Support IAM authentication in replication documents.
issue_body:
_Parent Issue: https://github.com/cloudant/cloudant-sync/issues/31_

Currently `db.creds['basic_auth']` returns `None` when using an IAM client . As a result, all replication documents created using IAM clients _don't_ contain source/target database credentials.

_Work Required:_
* Expose the IAM key via the client session ([here](https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/blob/2.7.0/src/cloudant/_client_session.py#L194)).
* Rework the [Replicator](https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/blob/2.7.0/src/cloudant/replicator.py#L77-L93) class to correctly inject the IAM key into the replication document (when databases are backed by an IAM client).
* Enable replicator tests for IAM clients (currently disabled).
* Add more unit tests as appropriate.

issue_title: ADMIN_PARTY environment setting is not needed in JenkinsFile
issue_body: I was poking around in the repo and stumbled upon the recently added JenkinsFile.  It looks like the `ADMIN_PARTY` environment variable is being set to `true` [here](https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/blob/master/Jenkinsfile#L13) which is unnecessary for Cloudant tests and only applies to running tests against CouchDB.  This does not cause any problem right now given the way the tests are written but if things change then this could become an issue in the future.  I would suggest to remove this line of code from the JenkinsFile since it currently does nothing and may possibly do some harm later on.

issue_title: Extend MXNet Distributed Training by MPI AllReduce
issue_body: ## Description Currently, almost all main-stream frameworks has mpi-all-reduce based training mechanism. When compared to Parameter Server solution, mpi-all-reduce has following strengths: 1) MPI Allreduce don't need extra server node and it can achieve the best performance which parameter server can reach in synchronous SGD multi-node training. 2) MPI Allreduce is easy for hardware deployment. (e.g. Don't need to consider the configuration of server:worker ratio.) ## What have you tried to solve it? We add one type of new kvstore (dist_sync_mpi) which extend MXNet distributed training by MPI AllReduce. In this type of kvstore, since there's no parameter server, we replace original kvstore apis push and pull with one single api pushpull. You can refer API Spec part in the design doc for details. Our design doc: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1e4anwDiS18cWP49FAghU6tqqdtnRKUcbNJJxvhIfvIA/edit#heading=h.t762l56r1094 ## Performance and Accuracy For Performance, our testing environment: Machine: SKX6148, Network: 10GbE, Topology: VGG16, Local Batch Size: 64 Single Node FPS: 27.3 (pic/second) For Accuracy, we compared top-1 and top-5 accuracy curve between 4 P100 GPU (with original kvstore dist_device) and 4 SKX6148 CPU (with new kvstore dist_sync_mpi). We found the accuracy curve is highly matched. (GPU: 130 epochs. CPU: 113 epochs.)

rm -rf venv
dotest 'mysql-python'
rm -rf venv
dotest 'mysqlclient'
rm -rf venv

issue_title: 'type' is forced by bravado-core to be present in definitions
issue_body:
If `definitions` object does not contain a `type` field, `validate` method throws up like so:

```
5	2015-08-28 00:47:14,022	y_app.yelp.web.pyramid_app ERROR   : 'type'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/nail/live/yelp/virtualenv_run/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyramid/tweens.py", line 21, in excview_tween
    response = handler(request)
  File "/nail/live/yelp/virtualenv_run/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyramid_swagger/tween.py", line 181, in validator_tween
    swagger_handler.handle_response(response, op_or_validators_map)
  File "/nail/live/yelp/virtualenv_run/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyramid_swagger/tween.py", line 412, in _validate
    return f(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/nail/live/yelp/virtualenv_run/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyramid_swagger/tween.py", line 535, in swaggerize_response
    response_spec, op, PyramidSwaggerResponse(response))
  File "/nail/live/yelp/virtualenv_run/lib/python2.7/site-packages/bravado_core/response.py", line 156, in validate_response
    validate_response_body(op, response_spec, response)
  File "/nail/live/yelp/virtualenv_run/lib/python2.7/site-packages/bravado_core/response.py", line 186, in validate_response_body
    validate_schema_object(response_body_spec, response_value)
  File "/nail/live/yelp/virtualenv_run/lib/python2.7/site-packages/bravado_core/validate.py", line 21, in validate_schema_object
    obj_type = spec['type']
KeyError: 'type'
```

From the [spec](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-spec/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#definitions-object) it does not look like 'type' key presence should be mandated. Instead it should be defaulted to 'object' is properties field is non-empty imo. Also, `swagger-converter` does not add `type: object` to any of the `definitions` after conversions.

The method `get_by_natural_key` can check if the `User.USERNAME_FIELDS` is a `hash_of` the value provided for login.

Example:
```python
class MyMixin:
    def get_by_natural_key(self, username):
        """Get user by comparing hashed email.

        `get_by_natural_key` is used to `authenticate` a user, see:
        https://github.com/django/django/blob/c5780adeecfbd85a80b5aa7130dd86e78b23e497/django/contrib/auth/backends.py#L16
        """
        return self.get(username_keyed__hash_of=username)
```

issue_title: Excluded models displayed as an underscore
issue_body:
I expected that excluding a model from `graph_models` would completely hide it from the graph. Instead, all my hidden models appear as an underscore when they are referenced by visible models.

Here is a simple example:

    # container/models.py
    from django.db import models
    
    
    class Foo(models.Model):
        name = models.CharField(max_length=30)
    
    
    class Bar(models.Model):
        name = models.CharField(max_length=30)
        foo = models.ForeignKey(Foo)

I graph that with this command:

    ./manage.py graph_models --pygraphviz container -o container.png

That produces the expected graph:

![good container]

When I exclude the `Foo` model with this command:

    ./manage.py graph_models --pygraphviz container -o container.png --exclude-models Foo

The model is replaced by an underscore. If the model is referenced by many other models, they are all linking back to this underscore.

![underscore container]

Is there a workaround for this? My current plan is to edit the dot file after it's generated, but I'd appreciate an easier alternative.

I tested this with django extensions 2.1.0.

[good container]: https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1639148/43482308-2d2ab91c-94bd-11e8-8055-334cb9d0d54e.png
[underscore container]: https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1639148/43482448-929630ba-94bd-11e8-9e9e-a0d21f9b191f.png

issue_title: Cannot create product catalogs with sdk v2.11.1
issue_body: In the last 2.11 release, you've removed the create_product_catalog method from the Business class. But not only that, you've also removed the api_create method from the ProductCatalog class, which means that in theory we are only left with remote_create() for creating product catalogs. But wait, according to https://developers.facebook.com/docs/marketing-api/reference/product-catalog the new product catalog endpoint is /{business_id}/owned_product_catalogs. Guess what, the same 2.11 release is referring to the old endpoint (i.e. /{business_id}/product_catalogs).

A quick debugging session indicated that pythons `selectors.py` tries to squeeze out a file descriptor from the ZMQ Socket object by calling `fileno()`, but the object does not have this method. I monkey-patched something like this into it:

```python
class Socket(_AsyncIO, _future._AsyncSocket):
    """Socket returning asyncio Futures for send/recv/poll methods."""

    _poller_class = Poller

    def fileno(self): 
        return self.FD
```

which resolved the issue. 

Any idea why this is not working out of the box? Any version mismatches?

issue_title: Bug: Linking stops after double entry in list of traceable items
issue_body:
If a list of linked items contains double entries (could happen by accident), the items after the double entry are not linked.

Example:
```
.. item:: SOME_DESIGN_ITEM Some design item
    :fulfills:
        SWRQT-MLX1
        SWRQT-MLX2
        SWRQT-MLX2
        SWRQT-MLX3
        SWRQT-MLX4
```

SWRQT-MLX2 is twice in the list. Because of this, links from and to SWRQT-MLX3 and SWRQT-MLX4 are not rendered.

issue_title: Implement Shamir's Secret Sharing for data key retrieval
issue_body:
Suggested by @jvehent.

Currently, every single master key has the capacity to fully decrypt the data key, and therefore the whole file. It would be desirable to have a way to require access to more than one master key in order to retrieve the data key. Using [Shamir's Secret Sharing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shamir%27s_Secret_Sharing), if one master key is compromised, the attacker can't get the data key, as they will need a quorum of keys to retrieve it. We can use [Vault's implementation](https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/tree/master/shamir) of the algorithm.

This should be relatively easy to implement by modifying the way we retrieve the data key from the encrypted file, `GetDataKey` in `sops.go`; and the way we write the data key to the encrypted file, `UpdateMasterKeys` in `sops.go`. 

The number of master keys required to form a quorum should probably be configurable, defaulting to half the number of keys plus one.

issue_title: Measure the WER increase vs comutation time decrease with a BiRNN stride of 2
issue_body:
Normally a RNN has time stride of `1` in time
```python
stride = 1
outputs = []
state = initial_state
with tf.variable_scope("RNN"):
  for time_step in range(0, num_steps, stride):
    if time_step > 0: tf.get_variable_scope().reuse_variables()
    (cell_output, state) = cell(inputs[:, time_step, :], state)
    outputs.append(cell_output)
```
However, a stride of `2` in time requires half the computation
```python
stride = 2
outputs = []
state = initial_state
with tf.variable_scope("RNN"):
  for time_step in range(0, num_steps, stride):
    if time_step > 0: tf.get_variable_scope().reuse_variables()
    (cell_output, state) = cell(inputs[:, time_step, :], state)
    outputs.append(cell_output)
```
Doing so with both RNN's of the BiRNN should cut computation by about a factor of two. However, it will also increase the WER.

The question to answer here is if this WER increase is worth the cut computation time.

issue_title: Use Nonce for validation in code flow.
match OIDC identity to Django user issues
issue_body:
Right now, mozilla-django-oidc matches an OIDC identity to a Django user via the Django user email field.

That's problematic for a couple of reasons:

1. the code is doing a case-sensitive exact match, so it's possible that could fail if the case ever changes (email addresses on some systems are case-insensitive)
2. the email field in the Django user table is not marked as unique and thus you can have multiple Django users with the same email field

I think to fix item 1, we want to do a case-insensitive match even though technically, email addresses are case-sensitive.

I think to fix item 2, we want to be using the username field of the Django user table and not the email field. Django 1.11's username field works fine with that. It means we don't have to generate usernames from email addresses. It means two users can't have the same email address. It solves a bunch of things for us.

Are there other issues we need to work around?

Are there better ways to solve this?
